q: HI: what storage structure gives Cassandra its high write throughput, replacing the B-tree?
options: A Log Structured Merge Tree (LSM tree)
A skip list
An inverted index
A hash table with chaining
why: HI: Cassandra uses an LSM tree instead of a B-tree, favoring write speed by treating most writes as append-only (Memtable then immutable SSTable).

q: HI: the purpose of a clustering key within a Cassandra primary key?
options: It picks which partition the row lives in
It determines the sorted order of rows within a partition
It enforces referential integrity across tables
It encrypts the row at rest
why: HI: a primary key is partition key(s) + clustering key(s). The partition key picks the partition; the clustering key sets sorted order within it (e.g. Discord ORDER BY message_id DESC).

q: HI: with replication factor 3, why does QUORUM for both reads and writes guarantee a read sees the latest write?
options: QUORUM forces all 3 replicas to respond
QUORUM needs 2 of 3 each, so read and write sets always share at least one node
QUORUM routes every request to a single leader
QUORUM disables eventual consistency cluster-wide
why: QUORUM = majority (n/2+1). For 3 nodes that's 2, so write touches 2 and read touches 2 of 3, guaranteeing at least one overlapping node that has seen the write.

q: HI: why did Discord add a bucket (fixed 10-day window) to the partition key of their Cassandra messages table?
options: To support JOINs between channels and authors
To stop busy-channel partitions growing unbounded and hitting large-partition problems
To enable strong consistency for reads
To reduce SSTables created during compaction
why: HI: partitioning only by channel_id let busy channels grow monotonically into large-partition problems. The 10-day bucket caps partition size and rolls over, while recent reads still hit one partition.

q: HI: which are true about how Cassandra handles writes and deletes? (select all)
options: A delete is recorded as a tombstone rather than removing data in place
Writes go to the commit log, then the Memtable, then later flush to an immutable SSTable
Each write updates the existing row in place inside the SSTable on disk
Compaction consolidates SSTables and removes tombstoned rows
why: HI: deletes are tombstones; writes hit the commit log (WAL) then the Memtable, later flushed to an immutable SSTable. SSTables are immutable (no in-place updates); compaction merges SSTables and purges tombstones.

q: HI: how do Cassandra nodes share cluster state (which nodes are alive, the schema)?
options: A central ZooKeeper coordinator
A peer-to-peer gossip protocol biased toward seed nodes
A primary leader broadcasting to followers
A shared SQL metadata table
why: HI: Cassandra uses peer-to-peer gossip with a bias toward seed nodes (guaranteed hotspots) so info reaches the whole cluster and no sub-clusters form. No single coordinator.

q: HI: maximum size of a single DynamoDB item, including all attributes?
options: 64KB
256KB
400KB
1MB
why: HI: each item has a primary key and can hold up to 400KB of data including all attributes.

q: HI: how do you choose eventual vs strong consistency for a DynamoDB read?
options: A table-level setting fixed at creation
Per-request via ConsistentRead=true on the read call
Automatically by item size
Globally per AWS region
why: HI: not table-level. Set ConsistentRead=true per GetItem/Query/Scan. Strong reads hit the leader, cost 2x the RCU (1 RCU/4KB vs 0.5), and aren't supported on GSIs.

q: HI: which correctly distinguish a GSI from an LSI in DynamoDB? (select all)
options: A GSI can use a different partition key; an LSI must share the base table partition key
GSIs support only eventually consistent reads; LSIs also support strong reads
LSIs can be added or removed any time; GSIs must be defined at table creation
An LSI is co-located on the base table's partitions; a GSI lives on separate partitions
why: HI: GSIs use a different partition key, live on separate partitions, eventual-only. LSIs share the partition key, are co-located, support strong reads, but must be defined at table creation (the reversed claim is wrong).

q: HI: using ProjectionExpression to return only a few attributes primarily saves what?
options: RCU cost — only projected attributes are read from storage
Network bandwidth only; the full item is still read and full RCU charged
Storage cost — unprojected attributes aren't persisted
Nothing — it's unsupported
why: HI: unlike SQL column selection, ProjectionExpression only cuts network bandwidth. The full item is still read and you pay full RCU on its total size — which is why large items should be normalized.

q: HI: roughly how many read/write capacity units does a single DynamoDB partition support?
options: ~3,000 RCUs and ~1,000 WCUs
~1,000 RCUs and ~3,000 WCUs
~10,000 RCUs and ~10,000 WCUs
Unlimited — no per-partition ceiling
why: HI: ~3,000 RCUs and ~1,000 WCUs per partition (~12MB/s reads, ~1MB/s writes) — useful for back-of-envelope partition-count math.

q: HI: an important caveat about DAX (DynamoDB Accelerator) and stale data?
options: DAX never caches reads, so staleness is impossible
DAX only auto-invalidates for writes that pass through DAX; direct writes to DynamoDB leave cached entries stale until TTL/eviction
DAX caches strongly consistent reads, which go stale
DAX needs no client change and is fully transparent
why: HI: DAX is read-through/write-through but only invalidates for writes through DAX. Direct DynamoDB writes bypass it, so entries stay stale until TTL/eviction. DAX also doesn't cache strong reads and needs the DAX client SDK.

q: HI: what index type does PostgreSQL create by default, including on a primary key?
options: A hash index
A B-tree index
A GIN index
An LSM tree index
why: HI: Postgres defaults to B-tree indexes (auto-created on the primary key), good for exact matches, range queries, and sorting when ORDER BY columns match the index order.

q: HI: why is read performance the first focus when evaluating Postgres for a social platform?
options: Writes aren't supported without extensions
In most apps reads vastly outnumber writes — users browse far more than they create
Postgres can't index write-heavy tables
Reads are billed separately
why: HI: reads vastly outnumber writes in most apps; users spend far more time browsing than creating, making read performance critical.

q: HI: a common interview trap with Postgres indexes?
options: Adding an index to every column
Using B-tree indexes for range queries
Indexing the primary key
Multi-column indexes for common query patterns
why: HI: indexing every column is a trap — each index slows writes, consumes disk, and may not even be used if the planner prefers a sequential scan.

q: HI social-media example: which operations have which consistency/integrity needs? (select all)
options: Creating a DM thread (thread, participants, first message) must be atomic
Like counts can be eventually consistent
Comments and follows need referential integrity
Follower counts must be strongly consistent across every replica instantly
why: HI: DM-thread creation must be atomic, like counts can be eventually consistent (a few seconds lag is fine), comments/follows need referential integrity. It doesn't claim follower counts need instant strong consistency.

q: HI warns candidates against which kind of statement about Postgres?
options: That it supports B-tree indexes
Overly broad claims like 'NoSQL scales better than Postgres' without understanding the nuances
That reads usually outnumber writes
That indexes speed up reads
why: HI: candidates get tripped up either diving too deep (MVCC, WAL) or making broad claims like 'NoSQL scales better than Postgres' without nuance. Interviewers want informed trade-offs.

q: HI: what fundamentally changes when you count clicks per user over the last 5 minutes vs processing each click independently?
options: You introduce state — each message must remember previous counts
You no longer need Kafka as a source
You can switch back to a stateless DB write
You eliminate windowing
why: HI: the 5-minute window introduces state because each message depends on previous ones, unlike the trivial stateless transform-and-write case.

q: HI: before reaching for a stream processor like Flink, what should you first ask?
options: Do I really need real-time latencies?
Can I afford more Kafka partitions?
Is my data structured or unstructured?
Should I use Spark or Hadoop for the sink?
why: HI stresses asking 'do I really need real-time latencies?' — many seeming stream problems reduce to batch problems (Spark/Hadoop), saving an ops headache.

q: HI: problems from holding 5-minute click counts as in-memory counters in your own service? (select all)
options: A crash loses all state, forcing slow re-reading of the whole Kafka topic
Scaling out requires complicated state redistribution to new instances
Out-of-order or late events can hurt count accuracy
Kafka can't deliver messages fast enough
why: HI enumerates crash-losing-state, state redistribution on scaling, and late/out-of-order events as the motivation for Flink. Kafka throughput is not cited.

q: HI: the role of the Job Manager relative to Task Managers in Flink?
options: The Job Manager coordinates and schedules; Task Managers execute the actual tasks
The Job Manager stores checkpoints and Task Managers only read them
Task Managers coordinate the Job Manager, which only persists state
Both run identical roles with no coordinator
why: HI: the coordinating Job Manager schedules work; Task Managers run it in task slots, which is where parallelism comes from.

q: HI: the basis for Flink's exactly-once processing guarantee?
options: Periodic checkpointing of state to a state backend
Re-reading the entire Kafka topic on every failure
Disabling watermarks so nothing is late
Holding all counters in volatile memory only
why: HI ties exactly-once to checkpointing state into a state backend, letting Flink recover after failure rather than replaying everything from scratch.

q: HI: why do watermarks matter in a Flink job?
options: They let Flink reason about event-time progress and handle late/out-of-order events
They encrypt the stream between operators
They replace the Job Manager
They define how many task slots each Task Manager gets
why: HI: watermarks track event-time progress so windows correctly account for late and out-of-order events.

q: HI: what core problem was ZooKeeper (2008) designed to solve?
options: Coordinating distributed systems — leader election, consistent config, failure detection
Storing large media blobs efficiently
A high-throughput message queue replacing Kafka
Batch analytics over historical data
why: HI frames ZooKeeper as distributed coordination: electing leaders, keeping consistent config, and detecting failures in real time.

q: HI: which primitives does ZooKeeper use to solve coordination problems? (select all)
options: A hierarchical namespace
Data nodes (znodes)
Watches
A SQL query engine
why: HI: ZooKeeper coordinates via a hierarchical namespace, data nodes (znodes), and watches. It has no SQL query engine.

q: HI: which universal distributed-systems problems does ZooKeeper teach you to solve?
options: Consensus, leader election, and configuration management
Image resizing, CDN routing, and compression
Garbage collection, JIT compilation, and threading
Sharding keys, hashing rings, and bloom filters
why: HI: learning ZooKeeper gives insight into consensus, leader election, and configuration management — the canonical coordination problems.

q: HI chat-app example: why does a single-server deployment need no coordination?
options: Both users connect to the same server, so delivery is in-memory and low latency
ZooKeeper is embedded in the single server by default
The server uses ZAB consensus even with one node
Messages are batched and processed offline
why: HI: when both users are on the same server, delivery is in-memory with low latency — coordination only appears once you scale out.

q: HI: which consensus protocol underlies ZooKeeper's strong consistency?
options: ZAB (ZooKeeper Atomic Broadcast)
Raft
Two-phase commit (2PC)
Gossip
why: HI's 'Consensus with ZAB' section indicates ZooKeeper relies on the ZAB protocol for its strong consistency guarantees.

q: HI: ZooKeeper's place in today's landscape?
options: Aged, with alternatives, but still central to the Apache ecosystem
The newest and fastest coordination service available
Fully removed from all major systems
Primarily a relational database now
why: HI: ZooKeeper (2008) has aged and faces alternatives, yet remains central to the Apache ecosystem in particular.

q: HI frames real-time updates as two distinct hops. What are they?
options: Client to LB, and LB to server
The first hop (server to client) and the second hop (source to server)
Browser to DNS, and DNS to origin
Read replica to cache, and cache to client
why: HI splits it into the first hop (server to client) and the second hop (source to server), each with different trade-offs.

q: HI: why does standard HTTP break down for real-time systems?
options: HTTP can't transmit binary data
HTTP is request-response — client asks, server responds, connection closes — so the server can't proactively push
HTTP is always slower than UDP
HTTP requires DNS on every request
why: HI: standard HTTP is request-response; it works for browsing but breaks when servers need to proactively push updates.

q: HI OSI layering: which protocol/layer/property pairing is correct?
options: IP at Layer 4 guarantees in-order delivery
TCP at Layer 4 is connectionless and spray-and-pray
UDP at Layer 4 is connectionless with no delivery/ordering guarantees
WebSockets at Layer 3 handle packet routing
why: HI: UDP is a connectionless Transport (L4) protocol with no delivery/ordering guarantees (spray-and-pray); TCP is connection-oriented; IP sits at L3.

q: HI: which client-server approaches does the first-hop section explicitly cover? (select all)
options: Long Polling
Server-Sent Events (SSE)
WebSockets
GraphQL subscriptions over gRPC
why: HI covers Simple Polling, Long Polling, SSE, WebSockets, and WebRTC. GraphQL subscriptions over gRPC is not one of the named protocols.

q: HI: for the second hop (source to the server holding the connection), which two PUSH approaches are contrasted?
options: Pushing via consistent hashes and pushing via pub/sub
Two-phase commit and gossip protocols
Write-ahead logging and change data capture
Leader election and quorum reads
why: HI details pushing via consistent hashes and via pub/sub (plus pulling via polling) as ways to route an update to the specific server holding a client's connection.

q: HI: how is SSE characterized relative to WebSockets?
options: SSE is full-duplex like WebSockets
SSE is an efficient one-way street (server to client); WebSockets are the full-duplex champion
SSE is peer-to-peer; WebSockets are server-based
SSE requires UDP; WebSockets require TCP
why: HI titles SSE 'The Efficient One-Way Street' (server-to-client) and WebSockets 'The Full-Duplex Champion'; WebRTC is the peer-to-peer option.

q: HI: how is contention defined?
options: When a database runs out of disk space
When multiple processes compete for the same resource at the same time
When a network partition splits a cluster
When a cache and database disagree on a value
why: HI: contention is multiple processes competing for the same resource at once (the last concert ticket), causing race conditions, double-bookings, and inconsistent state if unhandled.

q: HI Alice and Bob ticket example: why does the naive read-check-decrement oversell the seat?
options: The index on concert_id was missing
Bob reads the same count Alice read before Alice writes her update back, so both pass the check and both decrement
The UPDATE had a syntax error
The seat count was cached in a stale CDN
why: HI: Alice reads one seat, then Bob reads the same count before Alice writes; both check what they read, both decrement, dropping the count to negative one.

q: HI: which approaches to contention are covered as distinct named solutions? (select all)
options: Conditional Writes
Pessimistic Locking
Optimistic Concurrency Control
Eventual consistency via vector clocks
why: HI enumerates Conditional Writes, Pessimistic Locking, Optimistic Concurrency Control, Isolation Levels, and Distributed Locks. Vector-clock eventual consistency is not listed.

q: HI deep dive: the classic gotcha with optimistic concurrency is…
options: The thundering herd problem
The ABA problem
The split-brain problem
The dining philosophers problem
why: HI calls out the ABA problem: a value changes from A to B and back to A between read and write, defeating a naive version check.

q: HI: which failure mode is a deep-dive concern specific to pessimistic locking?
options: Lost updates
Deadlocks
Cache stampedes
Clock drift
why: HI has a dedicated deep dive on preventing deadlocks with pessimistic locking — holding locks across multiple resources can make processes wait on each other in a cycle.

q: HI's stance on when to reach for heavy contention machinery?
options: Always use distributed locks to be safe
It has a 'When NOT to overcomplicate' section, warning against over-engineering low-contention cases
Pessimistic locking should be the default everywhere
Contention can only be solved with a dedicated coordination service
why: HI's 'When NOT to overcomplicate' section signals you should match the solution to the actual level of contention rather than always reaching for distributed locks/OCC.

q: HI: which solutions does the multi-step article present?
options: Sagas, workflow systems, and durable execution
Sharding, replication, and caching
Load balancing, autoscaling, and rate limiting
Indexing, partitioning, and denormalization
why: HI centers multi-step processes on sagas, workflow systems, and durable execution (Single Server Orchestration, Event Sourcing, Workflows).

q: HI: the core design problem of naive multi-step orchestration?
options: It uses too much disk for logs
It interweaves system-level concerns (crashes, retries, failures) with business-level concerns, making the system brittle
It can't scale reads across replicas
It requires synchronous HTTP for every call
why: HI: organically patching processes interweaves system-level concerns like crashes/retries with business logic, making the design complex and brittle.

q: HI: which example workflow motivates the multi-step pattern?
options: A social media feed fan-out
E-commerce order fulfillment (charge payment, reserve inventory, create shipping label, wait for pickup, send confirmation)
A video transcoding pipeline
A real-time stock ticker
why: HI uses e-commerce order fulfillment: charge payment, reserve inventory, create a shipping label, wait for a human pickup, send confirmation — any step can fail or time out.
